subject: Mozi Money Minute: Orders of Magnitude
body:
Words I like: If you have < $10,000 saved up, don't buy crypto. Invest in the ability to make money. Then, you can restart the game as many times as you want.

Mozi Money Minute: Order of Magnitude

A lot of small businesses stay small because they play small. They think in increments rather than orders of magnitude. And to be clear - 10% improvements when you can get them are great. But there are diminishing returns. You can only get to 100%. In other words, you can't split test your way to a 10x. Sometimes, you gotta do something different.

Think about it like this…

You can't 100x your close rate, but you can 100x your traffic. And oftentimes, small businesses get so stuck in "optimization obsession", they miss the bigger picture.

Ex:

- Incremental: You improve your optin rate from 20% to 22%

- Order of magnitude: You make a promotion that generates 10X more leads

So the magic growth question to ask is: What would it take for us to 10x our__________________?

And the answer to that question is sometimes expensive, sometimes delayed, but almost always clear. Then, in answering that question, you then think "is there something else I could do for the same cost that could grow us even more?" And if the answer is no, you can ditch your optimization obsession, and make that one big move instead.

This is a bit of a more "strategic" email, but it was top of mind from business conversations I had this week. Sometimes the best move isn't doing what you do better, but doing better with what you move.

Reminder: 10x isn't 10% better - it's 1000% better. Invest accordingly.

As promised, money in under a minute.

A single 10x for any reasonable sized business can change your life forever. We've done it with our portfolio companies many times. If you'd like us to help you think through the 10x version of your business, and how to actually make it happen (from people who do this everyday), apply to see if you qualify for a workshop. We just opened up a new one on Jan 27/28. Last month's attendees averaged $2.9M in revenue - we turn away ~65% of applicants to keep the room quality high.

Actually write down the answer to the "What would it take?" question. It may change your life.

Alex
